title: ADMP Regulatory Trigger (Ambang ADMP)
description: ADMP regulation only applies where the use of automated decision-making (or profiling) results in legal effects (kesan undang-undang) concerning the data subject or significantly affects (kesan signifikan) the data subject. Verified against the JPDP ADMP Guideline (April 2026) Section 7 'Ambang ADMP'.

guidelineStatus.issuedReference: Garis Panduan ADMP, JPDP April 2026, Section 7 (Ambang ADMP)

dpoEngagement: DPO must be involved at the earliest stage of any ADMP design (§5.1). The DPO's involvement is not optional and not deferred to fieldwork.
dpiaTrigger: ADMP triggers a mandatory DPIA REGARDLESS OF NATURE OR EXTENT of the ADMP processing (§6.1: 'tanpa mengambil kira sifat atau tahap penggunaan ADMP'). The dpia/thresholds.json quantitative threshold (>10,000 subjects) is supplementary, NOT gating, where ADMP is in scope.

trigger.rule: Automated decision-making and profiling are regulated only if their use results in legal effects concerning the data subject OR significantly affects the data subject.
twoConditions: Decisions are being made by technological means without human involvement; AND
Such decisions may legally or significantly affect an individual.

legalEffects.definition: Something that affects a person's legal status or their legal rights.
examples: Denial of social benefit granted by law

significantlyAffects.definition: Produces an effect that is equivalent or similarly significant in its impact.
examples: Automatic refusal of an online credit application without human intervention
Automated e-recruiting practices without human intervention
decisionMustHavePotentialTo: Significantly affect the circumstances, behaviour or choices of the individuals concerned
Have a prolonged or permanent impact on the data subject
At its most extreme, lead to the exclusion or discrimination of individuals
exampleDomains: Decisions affecting access to health services
Decisions denying employment opportunity or causing serious disadvantage
Decisions resulting in price discrimination between data subjects
Decisions affecting access to education (e.g., university admissions)

outsideTrigger.rule: ADMP that has no real impact on the individual is not regulated.
examples: Researchers using automated profiling for sociological studies of city vs suburban residents (general trend analysis, no individual impact)
Customer 'stickiness' analysis used to improve services overall (not discriminating between user categories)

scopingFlow: 1. Identify all processing operations involving automated decisions or profiling
2. For each, assess whether it is fully or partially automated (issued §3.2 confirms partial automation is in scope; if a human only inputs data and the system decides, it remains in scope)
3. For each in-scope operation, assess whether it produces legal effects (kesan undang-undang) OR significantly affects (kesan signifikan) the data subject

4. If yes: ADMP Threshold (Ambang ADMP) is met — apply Section 7 notice obligation (mandatory: notify data subject of the FACT of ADMP) and Section 38 withdrawal of consent (where consent is the basis)
5. Provide explanation of logic/reasons/impact (§9.4) — permissive ('boleh'); may withhold confidential information, trade secrets, IP, or proprietary rights
6. DPIA is mandatory regardless of nature or extent of ADMP (§6.1) — the >10,000 subject threshold in dpia/thresholds.json is supplementary, not gating
7. Engage DPO from the earliest stage of design (§5.1)
